name: Adelita Grijalva
subject: Enough is enough. I'm suing.
body:
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
Hi, it's Adelita Grijalva — the Democratic Rep.-Elect being blocked from taking office.
Enough is enough.
I have filed a federal lawsuit to break Speaker Johnson's obstruction so that I can be sworn in immediately.
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
This has now become the longest swearing-in delay in recent U.S. history.
We're going to court because if they can silence one district, they can try to silence us all. It's an unlawful breach of our Constitution — and an attack on the democratic process itself.
I'm fighting back with every tool available. But I can't do it alone. I need you in this fight with me.
Will you split a contribution today between Oregon Sec. of State Tobias Read and my campaign as we work together to win this fight, defend our democracy, and end this obstruction once and for all? [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
DONATE NOW [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
It's been over a month since voters in Southern Arizona overwhelmingly elected me in a special election to represent them in Congress. We won by 40 points. The results have been certified. The people have spoken and they deserve representation.
This obstruction isn't just about politics. It's about a Speaker who's silencing an entire district because he doesn't like that my election threatens his razor-thin majority. It's an abuse of power that strikes at the foundation of our democracy and sets a dangerous precedent.
If that power is allowed to stand, any Speaker could override the will of the people and deny voters representation for months at a time.
This is a defining moment — not just for our district, but for our democracy. Every dollar we raise fuels this fight to hold Speaker Johnson accountable.
Please split donation right now between Tobias Read and my campaign to help us defend democracy and end this obstruction once and for all. [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
Thank you,
Rep.-Elect Adelita Grijalva
Arizona's 7th District
CONTRIBUTE [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/read-em-24-grijalva?refcode=251112em-rs]
https://www.facebook.com/TobiasReadOR [https://www.facebook.com/TobiasReadOR] https://twitter.com/TobiasRead [https://twitter.com/TobiasRead] https://instagram.com/tobiasreador [https://instagram.com/tobiasreador]

United States

It's an abuse of power that strikes at the foundation of our democracy and sets a dangerous precedent. If that power is allowed to stand, any Speaker could override the will of the people and deny voters representation for months at a time. This is a defining moment — not just for our district, but for our democracy. Every dollar we raise fuels this fight to hold Speaker Johnson accountable. Please split donation right now between Tobias Read and my campaign to help us defend democracy and end this obstruction once and for all. [] Thank you, Rep.-Elect Adelita Grijalva Arizona's 7th District CONTRIBUTE [] [] [] [] Friends of Tobias Read (5208) PO Box 42307 Portland, OR 97242 United States If you believe you received this message in error or wish to no longer receive email from us, please unsubscribe: .

name: Ruben Gallego
subject: Hey, quick question:
disclaimer_text: Paid For By Gallego for Arizona
body:
#

Peter,
I have a question for you, and I’d like an honest answer.
Do you think a person who is in tons of pictures and videos with Jeffrey Epstein, a person who partied with him, sent him a gross birthday letter, called him a “terrific guy,” allegedly spent several hours with a woman at Epstein’s own house, and is currently trying desperately to get members of the House to block the release of the Epstein files — **do you think that person should be the President of the United States?**
[YES](https://act.gallegoforarizona.com/signup/pet_em_251111_epstein_yes/?source=em251112-1-120&akid=4033%2E734936%2E0rzYru)
[NO](https://act.gallegoforarizona.com/signup/pet_em_251111_epstein/?source=em251112-1-120&akid=4033%2E734936%2E0rzYru)
Let me know.
Thanks,
Ruben

name: Norma Shulman and Bill Galvin
subject: Signature Deadline is Next Wednesday
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Massachusetts Democratic State Committee
body:
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP31/1/93809/images/Election%20Day%20Voter%20Reg%20New%20Header.png]
Hello Peter,
Time flies when you are collecting signatures!
This is a friendly reminder that the deadline to submit any signatures for Petition Y- Election Day Voter Registration is one week from today.
Local Submission Deadline:
📅 Wednesday, November 19
🕔 5:00 PM
All signatures must be submitted locally to the election officials to be certified in the city or town where they were collected. For example:
* If you collected signatures in Boston, you would submit those signatures to the Boston Election Department in Boston City Hall.
* If you collected signatures in Pittsfield, you would submit those signatures to the Registrar of Voters and Elections at Pittsfield City Hall.
If you have any questions, please email us at info@electiondayvoterregistration.com [info@electiondayvoterregistration.com] .
With the local submission deadline quickly approaching, now is the time to collect and submit any remaining signatures. Every signature gets us closer to ensuring that Massachusetts voters have the freedom to register and vote on Election Day.
Please submit any collected signatures to your local election office as soon as you can to ensure they’re certified on time.
Thank you for your continued dedication and hard work. You are making a difference, and we appreciate your help.
With gratitude,
Norma Shulman, Chair
Bill Galvin
YES on Election Day Voter Registration
444 Washington Street
Brighton, MA 02135

name: True the Vote
subject: We Dig Wells
body:
November 12, 2025 | Read Online (
https://click.convertkit-mail2.com/qdunm92pw8f7h4n8rmqslh82dzdkki4hkovx9/3ohphkh34gr35qar/aHR0cHM6Ly9ja2FyY2hpdmUuY29tL2IvNXF1dmg3aG43NW1kbGhwNXh4ZDUyYTkybG9sNDRhbmhnMG1wbw==
)

Hi Friend —

Last week I sent an email that sparked a wave of frustration.
Hard to tell exactly what the trigger was. Maybe my comments on
the recent clean sweep for Democrats in the election. Maybe the
lack of prosecutions related to everything that happened in the
2020 Election. Maybe just the general frustration of the times.

In any case, I understand. I feel the strain of these days too —
the sense that truth is slipping, justice is delayed, and our
nation is drifting into something unrecognizable. There is anger
in the air, and not without cause.

But yesterday something happened that brought me back to center,
and I want to share it with you.

On Tuesday, I attended a ministry event, where I had the
privilege of witnessing a pastor and father pass on the mantle of
a great work to his son. In the course of his comments, the
father spoke about wells, referred to time and again in Scripture
— how God used them to sustain His people, mark His promises, and
carry His legacy forward.

As I listened, I felt the Lord telling me, 'This is for you. This
is for your nation. In this season. Pay attention.'

True the Vote has never been just talk. We are a community. We
care deeply. And in days like these, letting off steam around the
"proverbial water cooler" is understandable. We can handle it. We
get the frustration, grief, even righteous anger. It’s good to be
honest. It’s healthy to vent.

But we cannot give up. We cannot quit the work of digging.
Because a people who stop digging wells — who stop preparing,
building, investing, shaping — will one day look for water and
find none. And that is how a people die.

Scripture says plainly: “My people perish for lack of knowledge.”
(Hosea 4:6). And part of knowledge is knowing when God is calling
us to pick up the shovel.

In Genesis, Isaac reopened the wells his father Abraham had dug,
only to find them filled with dirt by the Philistines. “And Isaac
dug again the wells of water... and he called their names after the
names by which his father had called them.” (Genesis 26:18)

Every time the enemy tried to choke off the source, God’s message
was the same: Dig again. And Isaac did. Again and again, until
water flowed.

There’s something in that for us.

A shallow well is easy work. It collects water quickly, but
without structure it collapses. Anyone can toss in dirt and muddy
it up. Anyone can foul it. It’s the kind of well built by people
who want water but not the work. Convenience without commitment.

But the deep wells — the lasting wells — are different. They
require excavation, shaping, lining the walls with stone, sealing
the gaps with mortar. Hard work. Intentional work. Work done with
purpose and love.

Those stones lining the walls are the programs we build, the
systems we strengthen, and the vigilance we keep.

And the mortar that holds it all together — is love. The love
Paul speaks of when he writes, “Above all these virtues, put on
love, which binds everything together in perfect harmony.”
(Colossians 3:14)

At True the Vote, our calling has always been to dig the wells
that help citizens support our elections. The kind of deep,
sustaining work that keeps the whole system from running dry. To
construct programs, tools, and safeguards, stone by stone, that
hold water.

I know you’re angry. I know you’re tired. I know the voices
calling for conflict are getting louder.

But the choice before us is as old as Scripture: War or wells.

And right now, in this season, I believe we are called to dig.

To dig deep. To dig with intention. To dig in unity, so that what
we build endures.

Some will say digging isn’t enough. It's not fast enough. It's
not fierce enough. But Scripture is clear: the people who dug
wells were the people who endured. The people who prepared.

If one day war becomes the only option, we will know it. And we
will be ready — not because we shouted the loudest, but because
we built the strongest. And we built together.

So for now, I am picking up my shovel. I am choosing to build.
And I’m asking you to build with me.

Wells that deliver living water and lasting freedom.

Deep digging has never been the work of the fainthearted. It
belongs to those with steady hands and clear eyes. And that is
you. That is us. I know it in my bones, now is the time to dig as
deeply as we can.

Thank you for your faith, your grit, your hope. These too are
stones. Love is the mortar.

And together, together, we will build as many wells as it takes.

And we will never quit.

May God strengthen all of us for the road ahead, and may He
continue to bless our beautiful America — for our children and
for their children — until all the work He’s entrusted to us is
finally complete, for our good and His glory.

Ever onward, with lots of mortar —

(

The kind of deep, sustaining work that keeps the whole system from running dry. To construct programs, tools, and safeguards, stone by stone, that hold water. I know you’re angry. I know you’re tired. I know the voices calling for conflict are getting louder. But the choice before us is as old as Scripture: War or wells. And right now, in this season, I believe we are called to dig. To dig deep. To dig with intention. To dig in unity, so that what we build endures. Some will say digging isn’t enough. It's not fast enough. It's not fierce enough. But Scripture is clear: the people who dug wells were the people who endured. The people who prepared. If one day war becomes the only option, we will know it. And we will be ready — not because we shouted the loudest, but because we built the strongest. And we built together. So for now, I am picking up my shovel. I am choosing to build. And I’m asking you to build with me. Wells that deliver living water and lasting freedom. Deep digging has never been the work of the fainthearted. It belongs to those with steady hands and clear eyes. And that is you. That is us. I know it in my bones, now is the time to dig as deeply as we can. Thank you for your faith, your grit, your hope. These too are stones. Love is the mortar. And together, together, we will build as many wells as it takes. And we will never quit. May God strengthen all of us for the road ahead, and may He continue to bless our beautiful America — for our children and for their children — until all the work He’s entrusted to us is finally complete, for our good and His glory. Ever onward, with lots of mortar — ( ) ( == ) ( ) ( == ) Update your email preferences ( ) or unsubscribe here ( ) © 2025 True the Vote 3120 Southwest Frwy Ste 101, PMB 19128, Houston, Texas 77098-4520

name: Mazie Hirono
subject: I voted no
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Mazie Hirono For Hawaii**
body:
|
#
---
Peter, On Monday, the Senate passed legislation to end Republicans’ painful shutdown and restore funding for SNAP and other programs. While I’m glad that families will begin receiving critical food assistance and that federal workers will receive the pay they deserve, **I voted no.** Here’s why: Affordable Care Act tax credits are set to expire at the end of the year, meaning health care costs will skyrocket for millions of Americans. I’ve fought alongside my fellow Democrats for months to prevent this from happening, but Republicans have refused to even _discuss_ extending the tax credits. Why? **Because they don’t give a rip about working Americans.** They’ve gutted Medicaid, fought to prevent hungry families from receiving SNAP benefits, and are perfectly fine with allowing ACA subsidies to expire. Republicans don’t have any problem finding the money to give large tax breaks to billionaires, but can’t be bothered to make sure Americans can afford their health care. **[I voted no because I refuse to vote for legislation that doesn’t extend ACA subsidies, but I’m determined to keep fighting to make sure millions of Americans aren’t forced to pay more for health care at the end of the year. Dear Peter,

We missed you at our Town Hall today! Our legal and policy experts discussed the future of reproductive rights—from the proposed VA abortion ban to crisis pregnancy centers and medication abortion. [](https://click.everyaction.com/k/119160327/582143254/2083279016?sourceid=1150971&utm_campaign=26emkge-1106aax-on&utm_medium=ea&utm_source=email&utm_content=townhall&amtOpts=&recurringAmtOpts=&am=&recurringAm=&is_optimized_ask=true&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9DRlJSL0NGUlIvMS85NTk4MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJhOTk2ZWY1Yy0wY2MwLWYwMTEtODE5Ni02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=NF0UCVh8plozRi7TEvFOeTTZCR5ZUCNogn9Hyveyc8s=&emci=2f8830e0-f3bf-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=a996ef5c-0cc0-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=954237) As promised, here's our final explainer—one that offers a bit of hope for the fight ahead. You asked: **How can shield laws protect access to abortion care?**

**Here's what you need to know:**
* * *
* **Anti-abortion lawmakers are reaching beyond their own state borders in attempts to ban abortion everywhere.** They've introduced bills to classify abortion pills as controlled substances and to criminalize providers who prescribe or mail medication abortion across state lines.
* **Interstate shield laws protect providers from being criminally targeted for offering care to out-of-state patients.** They also help keep patient medical data private and safeguard those who assist patients in accessing care.
* **By protecting providers, we protect patients—especially those living in abortion-ban states.** Telehealth and cross-state prescribing have been lifelines for people with disabilities, low-income women, people living in rural areas, and so many others.
* **Shield laws are now on the books in 18 states and the District of Columbia,** creating safe harbors preserving access to reproductive health care for thousands.

* * *
**The bottom line:** Anti-abortion extremists think they can impose their will on states where abortion is still legal—but they're wrong. Shield laws defend providers and protect care.

[**This is how we fight back: by refusing to give in and instead creating safe harbors where patients can access the care they need and deserve.**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/119160328/582143255/2083279016?sourceid=1150971&utm_campaign=26emkge-1106aax-on&utm_medium=ea&utm_source=email&utm_content=townhall&amtOpts=&recurringAmtOpts=&am=&recurringAm=&is_optimized_ask=true&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9DRlJSL0NGUlIvMS85NTk4MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJhOTk2ZWY1Yy0wY2MwLWYwMTEtODE5Ni02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=NF0UCVh8plozRi7TEvFOeTTZCR5ZUCNogn9Hyveyc8s=&emci=2f8830e0-f3bf-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=a996ef5c-0cc0-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=954237)

Because of your commitment to this fight, we’re giving you EARLY ACCESS to our Giving Tuesday match. [**Make your gift now and it will go 2X as far to protect providers, defend patients, and keep care accessible across the country.**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/119160329/582143256/2083279016?sourceid=1150971&utm_campaign=26emkge-1106aax-on&utm_medium=ea&utm_source=email&utm_content=townhall&amtOpts=&recurringAmtOpts=&am=&recurringAm=&is_optimized_ask=true&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9DRlJSL0NGUlIvMS85NTk4MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJhOTk2ZWY1Yy0wY2MwLWYwMTEtODE5Ni02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=NF0UCVh8plozRi7TEvFOeTTZCR5ZUCNogn9Hyveyc8s=&emci=2f8830e0-f3bf-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=a996ef5c-0cc0-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=954237) | [**UNLOCK EARLY ACCESS**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/119160330/582143257/2083279016?sourceid=1150971&utm_campaign=26emkge-1106aax-on&utm_medium=ea&utm_source=email&utm_content=townhall&amtOpts=&recurringAmtOpts=&am=&recurringAm=&is_optimized_ask=true&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9DRlJSL0NGUlIvMS85NTk4MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJhOTk2ZWY1Yy0wY2MwLWYwMTEtODE5Ni02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=NF0UCVh8plozRi7TEvFOeTTZCR5ZUCNogn9Hyveyc8s=&emci=2f8830e0-f3bf-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=a996ef5c-0cc0-f011-8196-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=954237 "UNLOCK EARLY ACCESS")

clean_body: Protecting providers. Protecting care. | | | ---|--- Dear Peter, We missed you at our Town Hall today! Our legal and policy experts discussed the future of reproductive rights—from the proposed VA abortion ban to crisis pregnancy centers and medication abortion. As promised, here's our final explainer—one that offers a bit of hope for the fight ahead. You asked: How can shield laws protect access to abortion care? Here's what you need to know: Anti-abortion lawmakers are reaching beyond their own state borders in attempts to ban abortion everywhere. They've introduced bills to classify abortion pills as controlled substances and to criminalize providers who prescribe or mail medication abortion across state lines. Interstate shield laws protect providers from being criminally targeted for offering care to out-of-state patients. They also help keep patient medical data private and safeguard those who assist patients in accessing care. By protecting providers, we protect patients—especially those living in abortion-ban states. Telehealth and cross-state prescribing have been lifelines for people with disabilities, low-income women, people living in rural areas, and so many others. Shield laws are now on the books in 18 states and the District of Columbia, creating safe harbors preserving access to reproductive health care for thousands. The bottom line: Anti-abortion extremists think they can impose their will on states where abortion is still legal—but they're wrong. Shield laws defend providers and protect care. Because of your commitment to this fight, we’re giving you EARLY ACCESS to our Giving Tuesday match. | "UNLOCK EARLY ACCESS" Together, we'll make reproductive freedom a reality for every patient, everywhere. In solidarity and hope, Elias Fox Bova Schmidt State Legislative Counsel "DONATE NOW" | | | | ---|---|--- The Center for Reproductive Rights uses the power of law to advance reproductive rights as fundamental human rights around the world. (C) Center for Reproductive Rights Center for Reproductive Rights 199 Water St. New York, NY 10038 United States

My fellow veterans deserve better than empty slogans and broken promises. = If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately: | | | = | = | | | = | = | | | = | = Thank you, Chris | Make sure this email goes to your inbox. body:
Hours ago, Boebert was called into the White House Situation Room for a closed door meeting that included Attorney General Pam Bondi and FBI Director Kash Patel.
| | | | | [](https://t7pkg9hbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001nCqmz_gbiPs43UpycVxn6qj4CkLmlWZ-irP2CVX2eUQGasU0msvS_oO1SiHMuwULkicbQNKD0jowa0_fm30YyYkqkMmZkynjenmezoM9DzT1z5cj_3kqknN5RtHT3uiZEQKpm230UjF86L1W3cUTWCFYkdlfaa4iAaeovUv5Flm_j0Zq_gLCepvA_nxQT9XALteSleWQTwWfS2Lov6zfhBtZTxr9lxGsBv66uwWU72g=&c=YQ4DNhIdLyZgz57E8tuu7IC3kjIMc1ByARwo59x3Lu4mA1-LiGhD7w==&ch=Q3jr_6YenP53YxIbcE5pzsmW8n9RFdVPF-JmvWxq9eCKB7bsQGnAiw==)

Peter,
Hours ago, Boebert was called into the White House Situation Room for a closed door meeting that included Attorney General Pam Bondi and FBI Director Kash Patel.
The meeting was a desperate hail mary by Trump to stop Congress from gathering enough signatures on a petition, which Boebert has signed, that would compel the House to vote on getting the Justice Department to release the FBI's Epstein files to the public.
Experts expect Boebert to flip given her vulnerable hold on her district, her abysmal fundraising, and her reliance on Trump. However, she now holds powerful cards and is using her signature to hold the White House hostage, extorting Trump for as much campaign cash as possible.
---
| [](https://t7pkg9hbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001nCqmz_gbiPs43UpycVxn6qj4CkLmlWZ-irP2CVX2eUQGasU0msvS_oO1SiHMuwULkicbQNKD0jowa0_fm30YyYkqkMmZkynjenmezoM9DzT1z5cj_3kqknN5RtHT3uiZEQKpm230UjF86L1W3cUTWCFYkdlfaa4iAaeovUv5Flm_j0Zq_gLCepvA_nxQT9XALteSleWQTwWfS2Lov6zfhMacdp2lk2vvqAgkrnoIJoQ=&c=YQ4DNhIdLyZgz57E8tuu7IC3kjIMc1ByARwo59x3Lu4mA1-LiGhD7w==&ch=Q3jr_6YenP53YxIbcE5pzsmW8n9RFdVPF-JmvWxq9eCKB7bsQGnAiw==)

Earlier today the judiciary committee released emails that described how Epstein was literally blackmailing Trump over his involvement with the women he trafficked.
Now that Trump has been decisively implicated in the scandal, Boebert can't put the genie back in the bottle. Her base is going to crucify her if she takes her name off that petition. And we are going to be watching and holding her accountable for how she actually votes.
When she revokes her signature and collapses the bipartisan effort to expose the Epstein Files, and/or votes against it on the floor, we need to be ready to rock and roll. We are preparing to hammer her over this relentlessly with attacks ads on tv, digital, and billboards.
This is about to be her most damning scandal ever and we will make sure every single one of her constituents knows about this betrayal. clean_body: It’s been a while since I heard from you. | | | | From Trump: You’ve got me worried I’ve been trying to reach you for DAYS, and I’m starting to think that you’re ignoring me. I really hope it’s because my messages haven’t been getting through to you. Because the Fake News has been saying that MAGA support is at an ALL-TIME LOW... But I know you haven’t abandoned me. I’m reaching out because YOUR NAME is at the top of my list of special MAGA Patriots, and I can’t afford to lose you. I know that you have never abandoned me and the MAGA Movement, but it’s been a while since I’ve heard from you... Please, I know that you’re a very busy person, but I could really use your response to some important questions that I have for you. I have always taken into account YOUR thoughts, and I need them now more than ever. THANK YOU. \- President Donald J. name: Chris Taylor for Justice
subject: Four quarters can make an impact
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Chris Taylor for Justice
body:
# [](https://act.chrisforjustice.com/go/18?refcode=em20251112-808&t=1&akid=809%2E294397%2ElefSgV)
Folks,
What if we told you that _four quarters_ could take on the tens of millions of dollars from right-wing special interests trying to buy a seat on the Wisconsin Supreme Court?
**It’s true.** Here’s why:
We don’t rely on right-wing billionaires or dark money groups.
Our campaign is powered by a grassroots team of thousands of supporters — people like you, pitching in what they can, whenever they can.
When you chip in $1, another person gives $1, then another, and so on. **Together, those small contributions build into a force powerful enough to beat back the far-right’s millions.**
That’s how we win — one person, one donation at a time.
[**Today, we’re hoping you’ll step up. If you believe in a fair, independent court that stands up for our rights and freedoms, chip in $1 to support Judge Chris Taylor:**](https://act.chrisforjustice.com/go/154?c0=em_ms_jct_20251112_1_ak.rrrCVF&refcode=em20251112-808&t=3&akid=809%2E294397%2ElefSgV)
**[Donate $1 ››](https://act.chrisforjustice.com/go/154?c0=em_ms_jct_20251112_1_ak.rrrCVF&refcode=em20251112-808&t=22&akid=809%2E294397%2ElefSgV)**
Thank you,
Team Taylor

**[Donate »](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/em_ms_jct_footer?refcode=em20251112-808&t=23&refcode2=809_294397_lefSgV&akid=809%2E294397%2ElefSgV)**

name: Team Fetterman
subject: John’s new bill (Social Security)
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Fetterman for PA
body:
[ https://act.johnfetterman.com/go/51612?t=1001&akid=10426%2E434096%2EnTToW3 ]John Fetterman

Folks, Americans deserve to retire with dignity – not spend their golden years scraping by. But right now? Too many seniors are struggling to afford basic necessities like food, medication, and clothing. That’s not right.

So John just introduced a new bill to help Social Security benefits keep up with rising costs and inflation – the Boosting Benefits and COLAs for Seniors Act.

It’s simple: Adjustments to benefits should keep up with the real cost of living. This year’s increase was just 2.8%. That doesn’t even come close to covering rising prices. 📈

John’s bill changes how benefits are calculated – it guarantees that benefits actually reflect seniors’ costs, like medical care and prescriptions. It’s fair. It’s overdue.

No one who’s worked their whole life should have to choose between paying for medicine or groceries. Folks earned these benefits.

👉 If you agree, will you be one of the 20,000 signatures we need to cosign John’s bill?

https://act.johnfetterman.com/go/52325?email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&t=1002&akid=10426%2E434096%2EnTToW3

Thanks for being with us,

Team Fetterman 💛

--------

This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com.

To unsubscribe from this email list, please click here: null

Paid for by Fetterman for PA.

This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is the most important way for us to reach you about opportunities to get involved. If you need to remove yourself from our email list, click here to unsubscribe: https://act.johnfetterman.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe?t=1003&akid=10426%2E434096%2EnTToW3

| | Dorian Gray glanced at the picture, and suddenly an uncontrollable feeling of hatred for Basil Hallward came over him, as though it had been suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips. The mad passions of a hunted animal stirred within him, and he loathed the man who was seated at the table, more than in his whole life he had ever loathed anything. He glanced wildly around. Something glimmered on the top of the painted chest that faced him. His eye fell on it. He knew what it was. It was a knife that he had brought up, some days before, to cut a piece of cord, and had forgotten to take away with him. He moved slowly towards it, passing Hallward as he did so. As soon as he got behind him, he seized it and turned round. Hallward stirred in his chair as if he was going to rise. He rushed at him and dug the knife into the great vein that is behind the ear, crushing the man’s head down on the table and stabbing again and again. There was a stifled groan and the horrible sound of some one choking with blood. Three times the outstretched arms shot up convulsively, waving grotesque, stiff-fingered hands in the air. He stabbed him twice more, but the man did not move. Something began to trickle on the floor. He waited for a moment, still pressing the head down. Then he threw the knife on the table, and listened. He could hear nothing, but the drip, drip on the threadbare carpet. He opened the door and went out on the landing. The house was absolutely quiet. No one was about. For a few seconds he stood bending over the balustrade and peering down into the black seething well of darkness. Then he took out the key and returned to the room, locking himself in as he did so. The thing was still seated in the chair, straining over the table with bowed head, and humped back, and long fantastic arms. Had it not been for the red jagged tear in the neck and the clotted black pool that was slowly widening on the table, one would have said that the man was simply asleep. How quickly it had all been done! He felt strangely calm, and walking over to the window, opened it and stepped out on the balcony. The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him.
---

Ivan Dmytrovych Sirko was born in the early seventeenth century, approximately between 1605 and 1610. This is evidenced primarily by the results of research on Sirko's remains, according to which the Cossack ataman was 70-75 years old at the time of his death in 1680. The first researcher of Sirko's biography, the famous Ukrainian historian Dmytro Yavornytskyi, suggested that the future ataman was born in Sloboda Ukraine in the settlement of Merefa (now the city of Merefa, Kharkiv region). However, this is not true. Merefa was founded only in 1658, i.e. when Sirko was in his late teens. However, Sirko and his family lived in Merefa for a long time in his house, and after his death, his wife and some family members lived there. The fact that Merefa in Slobozhanshchyna is associated with the name of the Cossack Ataman is evidenced by another name for the town recorded in some Ukrainian chronicles, Sirkivka. Where, then, should we look for Sirko's homeland? In our opinion, the sources give grounds to localize it in Eastern Podillia, namely in the Vinnytsia region. It is worth recalling that Sirko was a colonel in Vinnytsia (Kalnytsia) in 1658-1660. As you know, at that time in Ukraine, people who came from the local area were usually elected to such positions. Let us also recall a letter from Hetman of the Left Bank Ukraine Ivan Samoilovych to the tsarist voivode Prince G. Romodanovsky of April 20, 1675*, which recounts (in Russian) Sirko's own words: "And since I was born among the Poles, I want to die there." Thus, it is clear that Sirko was born on the Ukrainian lands that were under the rule of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, not Muscovy (the latter included Slobozhanshchyna). The historian Volodymyr Borysenko's assumption that Sirko's birthplace was the hundred-man town of the Bratslav regiment Murafa, a few kilometers from Kalnyk, is correct. Now it is the village of Zhdanov, Vinnytsia region. By the way, Murafa in the Slobozhanshchyna probably originated from this Murafa, because immigrants from the Right Bank of Ukraine to the Left Bank often gave the newly founded settlements the names of their native villages or towns. It is also worth noting Sirko's great interest in Keleberda, which he founded in 1675 on the left bank of the Dnipro. For a long time, he sought from the Moscow tsar the right to own this town or sloboda, as well as the strategically important crossing of the Dnipro near Perevolochna (Sirko wanted this crossing not for himself, but for the entire Zaporozhian Lowland Army). Contrary to popular belief, we believe that Sirko did not come from a Cossack family, but from a small Ukrainian Orthodox gentry. For example, we know the name of a Podillia nobleman, Voytekh Sirko, who was married to a certain Olena Kozynska. Sources mention them in 1592. It is possible that they were relatives of the Cossack leader. Perhaps it is no coincidence that the kings of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, writing to Sirko, called him "urodzon." The word "nobleman" evokes negative associations for many people due to stereotypes instilled in school textbooks of the Stalinist era. However, this approach is not always correct. The term "nobleman" is identical to the words "knight," "chevalier," "nobleman," etc. and was commonly used not only in Poland but also in Ukraine, Belarus, and the Czech Republic. If scholars in Russia, Poland, and the Czech Republic, having contracted vulgar sociologism, no longer accuse Pushkin or Bunin, Suvorov or Kutuzov, Žižka or Kostiushko of being of noble or gentry origin, why have attacks on the Ukrainian and Belarusian gentry continued until recently? Is it not because in this way the imperial "divide and conquer" was transferred to our long-suffering history? Was it not because it was necessary to impose a nihilistic attitude toward the history of Ukraine, to "prove" that Ukrainians or Belarusians were just dark labor without national consciousness, ethnographic material doomed to build other people's empires? Meanwhile, the Ukrainian gentry produced many outstanding sons of Ukraine, fighters for its freedom. Suffice it to mention the names of Kryshtof Kosinski, Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny, Ivan Sulyma, Ivan Mazepa, Bohdan Khmelnytsky, and his associates: Ivan and Danylo Vyhovsky, Ivan Bohun, Danylo and Ivan Nechai, Mykhailo Krychevsky, Jan Sokolovsky, Siluyan Muzhylovsky, and others. Sirko's appearance and personality do not always coincide with the conventional ideas about him that have developed in some works of art. The Cossack leader was slightly taller than average (174-176 cm), had regular facial features, and a straight nose. By the way, the great Relin intuitively caught this when he depicted him in the center of his painting "Cossacks Writing a Letter to the Turkish Sultan." It is interesting that the prototype for Sirko's image in the painting was the famous military leader General Dragomirov. Only one significant detail was not known to Repin and his consultant D. Yavornytskyi - a red birthmark on Sirko's lower lip on the right side. His contemporaries considered it a "sign of God" given to distinguish him from ordinary people. The Grand Hetman of the Crown, the future King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Jan Sobieski, who knew Sirko well and met him in Bratslav in 1671.

clean_body: Even members of Congress have been caught off guard, asking in hearings: “Who’s really in charge?”| | | | November 12 | | | | | Don’t Wait for Congress to Decide Your Financial Future | | | Dorian Gray glanced at the picture, and suddenly an uncontrollable feeling of hatred for Basil Hallward came over him, as though it had been suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips. The mad passions of a hunted animal stirred within him, and he loathed the man who was seated at the table, more than in his whole life he had ever loathed anything. He glanced wildly around. Something glimmered on the top of the painted chest that faced him. His eye fell on it. He knew what it was. It was a knife that he had brought up, some days before, to cut a piece of cord, and had forgotten to take away with him. He moved slowly towards it, passing Hallward as he did so. As soon as he got behind him, he seized it and turned round. Hallward stirred in his chair as if he was going to rise. He rushed at him and dug the knife into the great vein that is behind the ear, crushing the man’s head down on the table and stabbing again and again. There was a stifled groan and the horrible sound of some one choking with blood. Three times the outstretched arms shot up convulsively, waving grotesque, stiff-fingered hands in the air. He stabbed him twice more, but the man did not move. Something began to trickle on the floor. He waited for a moment, still pressing the head down. Then he threw the knife on the table, and listened. He could hear nothing, but the drip, drip on the threadbare carpet. He opened the door and went out on the landing. The house was absolutely quiet. No one was about. For a few seconds he stood bending over the balustrade and peering down into the black seething well of darkness. Then he took out the key and returned to the room, locking himself in as he did so. The thing was still seated in the chair, straining over the table with bowed head, and humped back, and long fantastic arms. Had it not been for the red jagged tear in the neck and the clotted black pool that was slowly widening on the table, one would have said that the man was simply asleep. How quickly it had all been done! He felt strangely calm, and walking over to the window, opened it and stepped out on the balcony. The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him. Now add this: reports suggest the government is preparing a dramatic move that could change the U.S. dollar as we know it. Even members of Congress have been caught off guard, asking in hearings: “Who’s really in charge?” Answer: not you. And not the millions of Americans whose savings are at risk of being wiped out when the dollar cracks under pressure. Meanwhile, smart money is already moving—out of paper, out of the banks, and into real assets like gold and precious metals. It’s the same playbook the elites use whenever chaos hits. The government may shut this loophole, and once they act—you won’t get a second chance. | | | 🟡 News From "American Alternative Assets" | (Sponsored) ---|--- Ivan Dmytrovych Sirko was born in the early seventeenth century, approximately between 1605 and 1610. This is evidenced primarily by the results of research on Sirko's remains, according to which the Cossack ataman was 70-75 years old at the time of his death in 1680. The first researcher of Sirko's biography, the famous Ukrainian historian Dmytro Yavornytskyi, suggested that the future ataman was born in Sloboda Ukraine in the settlement of Merefa (now the city of Merefa, Kharkiv region). However, this is not true. Merefa was founded only in 1658, i.e. when Sirko was in his late teens. However, Sirko and his family lived in Merefa for a long time in his house, and after his death, his wife and some family members lived there. The fact that Merefa in Slobozhanshchyna is associated with the name of the Cossack Ataman is evidenced by another name for the town recorded in some Ukrainian chronicles, Sirkivka. Where, then, should we look for Sirko's homeland? In our opinion, the sources give grounds to localize it in Eastern Podillia, namely in the Vinnytsia region. It is worth recalling that Sirko was a colonel in Vinnytsia (Kalnytsia) in 1658-1660. As you know, at that time in Ukraine, people who came from the local area were usually elected to such positions. Let us also recall a letter from Hetman of the Left Bank Ukraine Ivan Samoilovych to the tsarist voivode Prince G. Romodanovsky of April 20, 1675, which recounts (in Russian) Sirko's own words: "And since I was born among the Poles, I want to die there." Thus, it is clear that Sirko was born on the Ukrainian lands that were under the rule of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, not Muscovy (the latter included Slobozhanshchyna). The historian Volodymyr Borysenko's assumption that Sirko's birthplace was the hundred-man town of the Bratslav regiment Murafa, a few kilometers from Kalnyk, is correct. Now it is the village of Zhdanov, Vinnytsia region. By the way, Murafa in the Slobozhanshchyna probably originated from this Murafa, because immigrants from the Right Bank of Ukraine to the Left Bank often gave the newly founded settlements the names of their native villages or towns. It is also worth noting Sirko's great interest in Keleberda, which he founded in 1675 on the left bank of the Dnipro. For a long time, he sought from the Moscow tsar the right to own this town or sloboda, as well as the strategically important crossing of the Dnipro near Perevolochna (Sirko wanted this crossing not for himself, but for the entire Zaporozhian Lowland Army). Contrary to popular belief, we believe that Sirko did not come from a Cossack family, but from a small Ukrainian Orthodox gentry. For example, we know the name of a Podillia nobleman, Voytekh Sirko, who was married to a certain Olena Kozynska. Sources mention them in 1592. It is possible that they were relatives of the Cossack leader. Perhaps it is no coincidence that the kings of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, writing to Sirko, called him "urodzon." The word "nobleman" evokes negative associations for many people due to stereotypes instilled in school textbooks of the Stalinist era. However, this approach is not always correct. The term "nobleman" is identical to the words "knight," "chevalier," "nobleman," etc. and was commonly used not only in Poland but also in Ukraine, Belarus, and the Czech Republic. If scholars in Russia, Poland, and the Czech Republic, having contracted vulgar sociologism, no longer accuse Pushkin or Bunin, Suvorov or Kutuzov, Žižka or Kostiushko of being of noble or gentry origin, why have attacks on the Ukrainian and Belarusian gentry continued until recently? Is it not because in this way the imperial "divide and conquer" was transferred to our long-suffering history? Was it not because it was necessary to impose a nihilistic attitude toward the history of Ukraine, to "prove" that Ukrainians or Belarusians were just dark labor without national consciousness, ethnographic material doomed to build other people's empires? Meanwhile, the Ukrainian gentry produced many outstanding sons of Ukraine, fighters for its freedom. Suffice it to mention the names of Kryshtof Kosinski, Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny, Ivan Sulyma, Ivan Mazepa, Bohdan Khmelnytsky, and his associates: Ivan and Danylo Vyhovsky, Ivan Bohun, Danylo and Ivan Nechai, Mykhailo Krychevsky, Jan Sokolovsky, Siluyan Muzhylovsky, and others. Sirko's appearance and personality do not always coincide with the conventional ideas about him that have developed in some works of art. The Cossack leader was slightly taller than average (174-176 cm), had regular facial features, and a straight nose. By the way, the great Relin intuitively caught this when he depicted him in the center of his painting "Cossacks Writing a Letter to the Turkish Sultan." It is interesting that the prototype for Sirko's image in the painting was the famous military leader General Dragomirov. Only one significant detail was not known to Repin and his consultant D. Yavornytskyi - a red birthmark on Sirko's lower lip on the right side. His contemporaries considered it a "sign of God" given to distinguish him from ordinary people. The Grand Hetman of the Crown, the future King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Jan Sobieski, who knew Sirko well and met him in Bratslav in 1671. | | You are receiving this newsletter from Your Capital Minds (YCM), an esteemed member of the Stark Media LLC family. This week's capitulation by Senate Democrats has set off a firestorm of long overdue controversy. But why is anyone surprised at the weakness of elected Democrats?
We are living through an era of widespread cowardice.
But despite--the recent grassroots outrage--nothing about it is new. Democratic voters are outraged today by their party leaders making concessions to the GOP, as if they didn't build their careers by doing precisely that for decades.
### **What happened this weekend**
Seven Senate Democrats defected from the party line to accept GOP demands to support a continuing resolution in order to end the [government shutdown](https://substack.com/redirect/e59a8b8e-6f07-4100-817d-946a69991b17?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) without any decision to extend healthcare benefits that remain set to expire at the end of this year. They were joined by Sen. Angus King (I-ME), an independent who usually caucuses with Democrats but had voted for weeks to end the shutdown alongside his GOP colleagues.
As [summarized](https://substack.com/redirect/95948ceb-3764-43b3-8136-38fbce1ed3a6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) by _Politico_ , the compromise approved by the Senate "promises Democrats a December floor vote on extending the expiring Obamacare subsidies, though it's uncertain to pass the GOP-controlled chamber and Speaker Mike Johnson won't promise to bring up such a vote in the House."
_Thanks for reading Chronicles of a Dying Empire! You can help inform --and inspire--your friends by sharing this post._
[Share](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1018597&post_id=178651678&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTc4NjUxNjc4LCJpYXQiOjE3NjI5ODM2MDIsImV4cCI6MTc2NTU3NTYwMiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTEwMTg1OTciLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.nB7lEnZKcyIUu0BKhq3b1MxTWlFZGHICD9YBXkN-hrE)
Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) was appropriately skeptical of the tepid rationales offered by the defectors to explain their concessions. He described their move as "[a very, very bad vote](https://substack.com/redirect/4061280e-5232-4c08-83ea-5458de37fff1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," for both substantive and political reasons.
Substantively, Sunday's concession paves the way for millions of Americans to face escalated price gouging for healthcare. He [explained](https://substack.com/redirect/4061280e-5232-4c08-83ea-5458de37fff1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg):
> [F]irst of all...it raises health care premiums for over 20 million Americans by doubling, in some cases, tripling or quadrupling. People can't afford that when we are already paying the highest prices of the world for health care.
> Number 2, it paves the way for 15 million people to be thrown off of Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act.
Bernie also described America's disappointing defeat at the hands of timid careerists who made a "[horrific situation even worse](https://substack.com/redirect/9f29833f-75cc-4032-be5c-d0e5624acdff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)."
> As everybody knows, just on Tuesday, we had an election all over this country, and what the election showed is that [the American people want us to stand up](https://substack.com/redirect/4061280e-5232-4c08-83ea-5458de37fff1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to Trumpism, to his war against working-class people, to his authoritarianism.
> That is what the American people wanted. But tonight, that is not what happened.
The defecting Senators who sided with the GOP have [argued unpersuasively](https://substack.com/redirect/14b7d3b5-9649-447a-b04c-c9368c8372ca?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that the compromise they accepted was the best one available. None of them face re-election in the next electoral cycle, however, suggesting that their defection was coordinated and supported by other Senators who will face voters but couldn't accept the political risks of openly betraying them.
Sen. Dick Durbin (D-IL), who is retiring and supported the concession, [admitted](https://substack.com/redirect/14b7d3b5-9649-447a-b04c-c9368c8372ca?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that "This bill is not perfect," while claiming that "it takes important steps to reduce the shutdown's hurt."
Durbin's view was forcefully rejected by other Democrats representing his state. Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, for instance, [condemned](https://substack.com/redirect/95948ceb-3764-43b3-8136-38fbce1ed3a6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) the defection and GOP-enabling vote as a "complete betrayal of the American people."
That betrayal may ultimately achieve nothing, since multiple obstacles have emerged that could block the bill from ultimately passing both houses of Congress, including provisions that could perversely [compensate policymakers](https://substack.com/redirect/954e6572-76a5-4b32-a93a-11ff5b62be85?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) investigated by authorities for supporting the January 6, 2021 attack on the Capitol, and others that would [ban hemp farming](https://substack.com/redirect/ee33574d-12ee-445c-b306-fc2e2ee0f0c7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), which is supported by many GOP policymakers from Kentucky, in particular.
### **Confused policymakers**
On the one hand, some of the Senators who caved to GOP demands in order to end the shutdown may have been answering calls from their constituents.
[@roguednc](https://substack.com/redirect/62c735b9-0aad-4723-a2c6-1434ae423ec7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
[](https://substack.com/redirect/92b34ccf-849c-4ad1-af61-b9122d2ea54a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
RogueDNC on Instagram: "After more than a month of a government...
Three of the eight Senators had been voting alongside Republicans to end the shutdown since it began: King, John Fetterman (D-PA), and Catherine Cortez Masto (D-NV).

[Upgrade to paid](https://substack..cr-0Hh78ke4qUMTEJXH90XNtFW2dS9plgk_yv9VH9oI?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=178651678)
Cortez Masto was joined on Sunday by Nevada's other Senator, Jacky Rosen (D-NV). They may have been motivated by concerns about [travel delays and canceled flights](https://substack.com/redirect/0355cbf4-1314-4ded-8301-8c5aca0b1dd9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) resulting from [air traffic controllers not getting paid](https://substack.com/redirect/c73e36d4-c3c2-46d8-96a7-b9ff1d18a2dd?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) during the shutdown, which especially threaten their state given the role of its largest cities as tourist destinations.
But even to whatever extent one might support their reasoning, their concessions appear to have come [too late](https://substack.com/redirect/abe647ce-06b8-41c0-b093-ac3da6538a26?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to make an immediate difference. As [explained](https://substack.com/redirect/abe647ce-06b8-41c0-b093-ac3da6538a26?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) by _the Guardian_ :
> The Senate passed legislation Monday to reopen the government, but the bill still needs to clear the House and final passage could be days away. Sean Duffy, the transportation secretary, made clear last week that flight cuts will remain until the FAA sees staffing levels stabilize at its air traffic control facilities.
> Many planes also aren't where they're supposed to be, which could slow the airlines' return to normal operations even after the FAA lifts the order, said Mike Taylor, who leads research on airports and airlines at JD Power.
Regardless of how one evaluates the wisdom of conceding to GOP demands in order to support beleaguered air travel, their position reeks of corporate influence: the Senators who defected have been among the [biggest recipients](https://substack.com/redirect/55bc98d9-6060-4b74-b923-780a4d10d231?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) of campaign contributions from airlines, which have been [aggressively lobbying](https://substack.com/redirect/b5df58db-fa2c-46bb-9f17-42372560e5a9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to end the shutdown.
Sen. Tim Kaine (D-VA) argued that his vote would help support his constituents, who include a large number of federal workers. He explained his vote by [focusing](https://substack.com/redirect/8be64e74-9d01-4051-b020-b2c5fd0c3f5d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) on its provisions to "protect federal workers from baseless firings, reinstate those who have been wrongfully terminated during the shutdown, and ensure federal workers receive back pay."
Those interests are important, but narrowly shared, offering a meager gain for the profound loss implicit in giving away the Senate's approval. Ultimately, given Kaine's role as Hillary Clinton's running mate in 2016, it should [not surprise anyone](https://substack.com/redirect/dd93a90e-2390-4ddb-aa39-6bfb818d5082?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to see him making tactical concessions to the right wing.
[Share](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1018597&post_id=178651678&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTc4NjUxNjc4LCJpYXQiOjE3NjI5ODM2MDIsImV4cCI6MTc2NTU3NTYwMiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTEwMTg1OTciLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.nB7lEnZKcyIUu0BKhq3b1MxTWlFZGHICD9YBXkN-hrE)
The last two Senators to join the group of sellouts both represent New Hampshire: Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH) and Maggie Hassan (D-NH). Neither of them can claim a rationale even as weak as those of Kaine, Cortez Masto, or Rosen. Shaheen was even [publicly refuted](https://substack.com/redirect/6f7e216a-cb11-44a8-9a80-3d3e85dce1f0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) by her own daughter, Stefany Shaheen, who is running for Congress and therefore more accountable to public opinion.
As if the concessions by these figures did not do enough damage to their own party, they also emboldened the GOP, leading Republican Senators to expand their demands yesterday. After initially pledging to allow a vote on healthcare subsidies if the 7 Democrats defected, GOP leaders [changed their minds](https://substack.com/redirect/b24cd757-8663-4bb2-a6cc-793a4aaca31e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) after Democrats complied to instead demand tighter restrictions enforcing the Hyde amendment, which was passed in 2010 to prevent Obamacare funds from being used for family planning services.
It feels particularly maddening to watch the Hyde Amendment become a fulcrum for this debate, after running for Congress in part to [repeal it](https://substack.com/redirect/d273e8d3-1da1-4d69-a949-bcc77e63a9d6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) against an [oligarch](https://substack.com/redirect/de79ef48-be56-470c-ab0c-095382ee6c5d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) who [endorsed](https://substack.com/redirect/6f6b6e94-4492-446a-a4da-65b9111fd529?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) anti-choice Democrats but was enabled by journalists to duck debates, and any public accountability for her repeated [betrayals](https://substack.com/redirect/47dba3f0-9710-4906-8ef0-53d54ab68de8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) of her constituents.
### **Long overdue calls for transition**
In the wake of the Senate votes ending the shutdown, the criticism of institutional Democrats has been [swift, consistent, and loud](https://substack.com/redirect/9f29833f-75cc-4032-be5c-d0e5624acdff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
For example, [Indivisible](https://substack.com/redirect/3080d11c-4334-4241-9c4a-51e6766f8251?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), a grassroots network that helped organize the recent No Kings rallies, as well as groups in congressional districts around the country engaging in grassroots lobbying on progressive issues, [laid blame](https://substack.com/redirect/e51d46c3-10a7-4546-a450-f680eb4bdea8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) where due:
> Despite his no vote, the entire political world knows this is a direct result of Chuck Schumer's leadership. He either blessed this surrender, or was incapable of leading his caucus to hold the line.
>
> Schumer's second massive capitulation to the Trump regime has shown Trump that he can continue to plow forward with his authoritarian agenda without meaningful opposition from Congress. It's shown him that inflicting pain on Americans helps him achieve his agenda.
> And it's shown Americans intent on resisting Trumpism that we desperately need new leadership in the Democratic Party.
Having run to replace a leading Democrat in the House whose concessions paved the path for this continuing pattern, I could not agree more.
Even Schumer's colleagues were sharply critical. Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) described the concession as "a [terrible mistake](https://substack.com/redirect/9f29833f-75cc-4032-be5c-d0e5624acdff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," while Sen. Chris Murphy (D-CT) went further to [explain](https://substack.com/redirect/9f29833f-75cc-4032-be5c-d0e5624acdff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that:
> The people were on our side. We were building momentum to help save our democracy. We could have won--the premium increase notices were just starting...And giving in now will embolden [Trump]. Things will likely get worse."
Some Democrats in Congress have responded by calling for Sen Chuck Schumer (D-NY) to either step down as Senate Minority Leader, or instead to lose his seat in the Senate to make way for another voice more committed to the job.
For instance, Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA) said that, "This is a defining moment for the party. We need [new faces with bold new ideas](https://substack.com/redirect/9f29833f-75cc-4032-be5c-d0e5624acdff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). The American people are tired of a failed status quo." He also argued that, "Senator Schumer is no longer effective and [should be replaced](https://substack.com/redirect/70221bf6-c707-4e33-9bb6-420ad8bf11ff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). If you can't lead the fight to stop healthcare premiums from skyrocketing for Americans, what will you fight for?"
### **Why do we keep watching this pattern?**
Rep. Khanna's question was thoughtful, but also dramatically underinclusive.
Millions of voters asked the same question when Joe Biden and Kamala Harris [refused](https://substack.com/redirect/d7ddf607-c95d-4846-8630-cb082b60f548?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to condemn a genocide in Gaza enabled by their support for Israel, only to receive silence in response. One of them was author Ta-Nehisi Coates, who [put it simply](https://substack.com/redirect/9a6a10fa-592c-4ff2-b38c-41b7b16f19ca?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg):
> [I]f you can't draw the line at genocide, you probably can't draw the line at democracy.
That [failure](https://substack.com/redirect/d7ddf607-c95d-4846-8630-cb082b60f548?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) of Democrats to stand up for the most basic principles [played a critical role](https://substack.com/redirect/dd93a90e-2390-4ddb-aa39-6bfb818d5082?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) in returning Trump to the White House.
[Share](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1018597&post_id=178651678&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTc4NjUxNjc4LCJpYXQiOjE3NjI5ODM2MDIsImV4cCI6MTc2NTU3NTYwMiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTEwMTg1OTciLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.nB7lEnZKcyIUu0BKhq3b1MxTWlFZGHICD9YBXkN-hrE)
It's more than just the co-optation of powerful individuals: the party's structure also enables these patterns, by creating [elites within the party](https://substack.com/redirect/0917642f-8f27-46ad-a140-34dcfba7a52e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) whose career interests override the needs of grassroots supporters, as well as the vision of people who have offered better alternatives. The result, beyond a painfully incapable gerontocracy, is an [institutional rot](https://substack.com/redirect/e03760c6-1c6f-462f-9b97-bb1457c61558?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) with intergenerational roots.
It's worth noting that the [press](https://substack.com/redirect/d78e6a2b-10dc-4732-89e1-20031d43d08b?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) further enables the pattern, in several ways. Not only do reporters ignore electoral challengers and smear them relentlessly, they also grant undeserved credibility to elected officials even if they duck debates, betray their own rhetoric in action, or unconstitutionally [profit from their offices](https://substack.com/redirect/2a948e5a-59d5-4c43-822a-d4664a6d32b5?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
### **We 've been here before**
I dedicated nearly a decade to replacing a perfect example of that pattern: former House Speaker [Nancy Pelosi](https://substack.com/redirect/de79ef48-be56-470c-ab0c-095382ee6c5d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
Despite ducking debates, routinely selling out positions supported by grassroots Democrats, and unconstitutionally profiting from her office through decades of continuing insider trading, Pelosi has remained entrenched for nearly 40 years by playing the press like a fiddle. Reporters, eager to gain access to influential figures, have constructed a [cult of personality](https://substack.com/redirect/d79b3dfa-4b21-47d7-a6cb-573f0abe6250?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) surrounding Pelosi antithetical to their ethical obligations to hold power accountable.
As a result of their [longstanding co-optation](https://substack.com/redirect/d78e6a2b-10dc-4732-89e1-20031d43d08b?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), legions of liberals can't recognize a conservative even if she stares them in the face, [denies](https://substack.com/redirect/ffe07f39-8873-403f-b555-6d008d5279a1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) them healthcare (sound [familiar](https://substack.com/redirect/2384f8b0-e8ae-4555-913c-1390ec3805ce?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)?), ducks debates for [40 years](https://substack.com/redirect/fb819ee2-7b96-4cc0-94eb-43ff4c28a5f1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) at a time, relies on a "[civic lynching](https://substack.com/redirect/a9e22033-eebc-495d-a7ab-5563900e4715?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)" orchestrated across her party to retain her seat, and [accuses](https://substack.com/redirect/47dba3f0-9710-4906-8ef0-53d54ab68de8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) her own constituents of being foreign agents.
Pelosi is, sadly, far from alone. The ranks of powerful figures willing to accommodate fascism include those leading any number of [news](https://substack.com/redirect/bc1a9ee6-98b6-4c50-964e-e1140cffd5f6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) organizations, [corporate](https://substack.com/redirect/96ccc82a-ef59-4939-bd62-b1e034540c1d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) brands, [law](https://substack.com/redirect/2b9607ae-225a-47c9-bee5-f9e8681f5333?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) firms, and even many [universities](https://substack.com/redirect/40423132-7f69-4cff-a239-30c2e9c9f2db?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
We are living through an era of widespread cowardice.
But--despite the recent grassroots outrage--nothing about it is new. Democratic voters are outraged today by their party leaders making concessions to the GOP, as if they didn't build their careers by doing precisely that for decades.
In the face of such complicity, I feel all the more proud to have offered a meaningful [alternative](https://substack.com/redirect/efdc3079-b454-4207-aff4-c25c5283748d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) at a time when it could have mattered--and grateful for the readers who enable me to continue writing and educating today.

clean_body: Listen now (13 mins) | The Democratic Party has spent decades making concessions to the GOP, but a new generation of emboldened voters might be done settling for scraps | | ---|---|--- | | | Forwarded this email? ? for more ? | | | | | | | | | | | | ---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---]() | | The Democratic Party has spent decades making concessions to the GOP, but a new generation of emboldened voters might be done settling for scraps | | | Nov 12 | | | | | | | | | | | | This week's capitulation by Senate Democrats has set off a firestorm of long overdue controversy. But why is anyone surprised at the weakness of elected Democrats? We are living through an era of widespread cowardice. But despite--the recent grassroots outrage--nothing about it is new. Democratic voters are outraged today by their party leaders making concessions to the GOP, as if they didn't build their careers by doing precisely that for decades. What happened this weekend Seven Senate Democrats defected from the party line to accept GOP demands to support a continuing resolution in order to end the without any decision to extend healthcare benefits that remain set to expire at the end of this year. They were joined by Sen. Angus King (I-ME), an independent who usually caucuses with Democrats but had voted for weeks to end the shutdown alongside his GOP colleagues. As by Politico , the compromise approved by the Senate "promises Democrats a December floor vote on extending the expiring Obamacare subsidies, though it's uncertain to pass the GOP-controlled chamber and Speaker Mike Johnson won't promise to bring up such a vote in the House." Thanks for reading Chronicles of a Dying Empire! You can help inform --and inspire--your friends by sharing this post. Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) was appropriately skeptical of the tepid rationales offered by the defectors to explain their concessions. He described their move as "," for both substantive and political reasons. Substantively, Sunday's concession paves the way for millions of Americans to face escalated price gouging for healthcare. He : [F]irst of all...it raises health care premiums for over 20 million Americans by doubling, in some cases, tripling or quadrupling. People can't afford that when we are already paying the highest prices of the world for health care. Number 2, it paves the way for 15 million people to be thrown off of Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act. Bernie also described America's disappointing defeat at the hands of timid careerists who made a "." As everybody knows, just on Tuesday, we had an election all over this country, and what the election showed is that to Trumpism, to his war against working-class people, to his authoritarianism. That is what the American people wanted. But tonight, that is not what happened. The defecting Senators who sided with the GOP have that the compromise they accepted was the best one available. None of them face re-election in the next electoral cycle, however, suggesting that their defection was coordinated and supported by other Senators who will face voters but couldn't accept the political risks of openly betraying them. Sen. Dick Durbin (D-IL), who is retiring and supported the concession, that "This bill is not perfect," while claiming that "it takes important steps to reduce the shutdown's hurt." Durbin's view was forcefully rejected by other Democrats representing his state. Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, for instance, the defection and GOP-enabling vote as a "complete betrayal of the American people." That betrayal may ultimately achieve nothing, since multiple obstacles have emerged that could block the bill from ultimately passing both houses of Congress, including provisions that could perversely investigated by authorities for supporting the January 6, 2021 attack on the Capitol, and others that would , which is supported by many GOP policymakers from Kentucky, in particular. Confused policymakers On the one hand, some of the Senators who caved to GOP demands in order to end the shutdown may have been answering calls from their constituents. RogueDNC on Instagram: "After more than a month of a government... Three of the eight Senators had been voting alongside Republicans to end the shutdown since it began: King, John Fetterman (D-PA), and Catherine Cortez Masto (D-NV). Chronicles of a Dying Empire is a free publication made possible by readers. Our next paid subscriber will be the 72nd reader making possible writing that 21,000 other readers read--and learn from--for free. Cortez Masto was joined on Sunday by Nevada's other Senator, Jacky Rosen (D-NV). They may have been motivated by concerns about resulting from during the shutdown, which especially threaten their state given the role of its largest cities as tourist destinations. But even to whatever extent one might support their reasoning, their concessions appear to have come to make an immediate difference. As by the Guardian : The Senate passed legislation Monday to reopen the government, but the bill still needs to clear the House and final passage could be days away. Sean Duffy, the transportation secretary, made clear last week that flight cuts will remain until the FAA sees staffing levels stabilize at its air traffic control facilities. Many planes also aren't where they're supposed to be, which could slow the airlines' return to normal operations even after the FAA lifts the order, said Mike Taylor, who leads research on airports and airlines at JD Power. Regardless of how one evaluates the wisdom of conceding to GOP demands in order to support beleaguered air travel, their position reeks of corporate influence: the Senators who defected have been among the of campaign contributions from airlines, which have been to end the shutdown. Sen. Tim Kaine (D-VA) argued that his vote would help support his constituents, who include a large number of federal workers. He explained his vote by on its provisions to "protect federal workers from baseless firings, reinstate those who have been wrongfully terminated during the shutdown, and ensure federal workers receive back pay." Those interests are important, but narrowly shared, offering a meager gain for the profound loss implicit in giving away the Senate's approval. Ultimately, given Kaine's role as Hillary Clinton's running mate in 2016, it should to see him making tactical concessions to the right wing. The last two Senators to join the group of sellouts both represent New Hampshire: Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH) and Maggie Hassan (D-NH). Neither of them can claim a rationale even as weak as those of Kaine, Cortez Masto, or Rosen. Shaheen was even by her own daughter, Stefany Shaheen, who is running for Congress and therefore more accountable to public opinion. As if the concessions by these figures did not do enough damage to their own party, they also emboldened the GOP, leading Republican Senators to expand their demands yesterday. After initially pledging to allow a vote on healthcare subsidies if the 7 Democrats defected, GOP leaders after Democrats complied to instead demand tighter restrictions enforcing the Hyde amendment, which was passed in 2010 to prevent Obamacare funds from being used for family planning services. It feels particularly maddening to watch the Hyde Amendment become a fulcrum for this debate, after running for Congress in part to against an who anti-choice Democrats but was enabled by journalists to duck debates, and any public accountability for her repeated of her constituents. Long overdue calls for transition In the wake of the Senate votes ending the shutdown, the criticism of institutional Democrats has been . For example, , a grassroots network that helped organize the recent No Kings rallies, as well as groups in congressional districts around the country engaging in grassroots lobbying on progressive issues, where due: Despite his no vote, the entire political world knows this is a direct result of Chuck Schumer's leadership. He either blessed this surrender, or was incapable of leading his caucus to hold the line. Schumer's second massive capitulation to the Trump regime has shown Trump that he can continue to plow forward with his authoritarian agenda without meaningful opposition from Congress. It's shown him that inflicting pain on Americans helps him achieve his agenda. And it's shown Americans intent on resisting Trumpism that we desperately need new leadership in the Democratic Party. Having run to replace a leading Democrat in the House whose concessions paved the path for this continuing pattern, I could not agree more. Even Schumer's colleagues were sharply critical. Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) described the concession as "a ," while Sen. Chris Murphy (D-CT) went further to that: The people were on our side. We were building momentum to help save our democracy. We could have won--the premium increase notices were just starting...And giving in now will embolden [Trump]. Things will likely get worse." Some Democrats in Congress have responded by calling for Sen Chuck Schumer (D-NY) to either step down as Senate Minority Leader, or instead to lose his seat in the Senate to make way for another voice more committed to the job. For instance, Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA) said that, "This is a defining moment for the party. We need . The American people are tired of a failed status quo." He also argued that, "Senator Schumer is no longer effective and . If you can't lead the fight to stop healthcare premiums from skyrocketing for Americans, what will you fight for?" Why do we keep watching this pattern? Rep. Khanna's question was thoughtful, but also dramatically underinclusive. Millions of voters asked the same question when Joe Biden and Kamala Harris to condemn a genocide in Gaza enabled by their support for Israel, only to receive silence in response. One of them was author Ta-Nehisi Coates, who : [I]f you can't draw the line at genocide, you probably can't draw the line at democracy. That of Democrats to stand up for the most basic principles in returning Trump to the White House. It's more than just the co-optation of powerful individuals: the party's structure also enables these patterns, by creating whose career interests override the needs of grassroots supporters, as well as the vision of people who have offered better alternatives. The result, beyond a painfully incapable gerontocracy, is an with intergenerational roots. It's worth noting that the further enables the pattern, in several ways. Not only do reporters ignore electoral challengers and smear them relentlessly, they also grant undeserved credibility to elected officials even if they duck debates, betray their own rhetoric in action, or unconstitutionally . We 've been here before I dedicated nearly a decade to replacing a perfect example of that pattern: former House Speaker . Despite ducking debates, routinely selling out positions supported by grassroots Democrats, and unconstitutionally profiting from her office through decades of continuing insider trading, Pelosi has remained entrenched for nearly 40 years by playing the press like a fiddle. Woof! Hi there!

I’m Peanut – Carol’s dog and best friend.

Carol’s been saying something about a big mid-quarter deadline coming up in just three days. I don’t really know what that means, but I do know she’s been pacing around, constantly on the phone, and saying things like, “We’re so close!”

She told me this deadline helps keep her campaign strong – to fight Republican extremism and deliver results for working families in PA-07. I told her I’d do my part and send an email to all her friends.

Friend, I voted no on the so-called “deal” in the Senate to end the shutdown. As much as I want to see the shutdown come to an end, Americans cannot afford a plan that doesn’t extend the ACA tax credits. We can’t give in to empty promises. Right now, too many families are choosing between groceries, rent, and health care. **_That is unacceptable._** Because of our fight, we moved the debate forward. But Senate and House Republicans, backed by Donald Trump, walked away from a real solution to reduce premiums and extend the ACA tax credits that help families make ends meet. **I will not accept this cruelty.** I am fighting to permanently extend the tax credits, bring down health care costs, and stop policies that drive up prices at the grocery store and the gas pump. But we cannot win this fight without resources on the ground to organize and elect leaders who won’t back down from this fight.
